summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is 'ferromagnetism'?

Ferromagnetic materials have cooperative alignment of atomic magnetic moments in domains, producing strong, permanent magnetization.

What is the 'Curie temperature'?

Above the Curie temperature, thermal energy overcomes the exchange interaction that aligns domains, and the material becomes paramagnetic.

What is 'paramagnetism'?

Paramagnetic materials have atoms with permanent magnetic moments that partially align with an external field, producing weak attraction.

What is 'magnetic permeability'?

Permeability mu relates B to H: B = mu*H. High permeability means the material greatly enhances the magnetic field.

What is the 'Meissner effect'?

The Meissner effect is the defining property of superconductors: they expel all B from their bulk, enabling frictionless magnetic levitation.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A maglev train uses superconducting magnets for levitation. The primary advantage of superconductors over ordinary electromagnets is:

Superconductors have zero resistance, so once current is established, it flows indefinitely without energy input, maintaining strong persistent fields.
